sample.title: A Frozen Rooster
sample.plot: The movie centers on Toto's family journeying to the South Pole to return a polar bear and Spanish penguins home, facing obstacles that highlight the value of teamwork along the way.

categories.label: research stations surviving by constant resupply
categories.description: Antarctic life depends on supplies brought in from far away, because almost nothing needed for shelter, fuel, food, or medicine can be made there. What you'll see: cargo planes, icebreakers, packed storerooms, fuel drums, and every task measured against weather and limited provisions.

categories.label: antarctica governed by treaties and science
categories.description: No single country rules the continent outright, and daily presence is shaped by international agreements that privilege research, cooperation, and protected zones. What you'll see: national flags at stations, scientists from different countries, mapped boundaries, and rules about where people may land, travel, or disturb the ground.

categories.label: distance bridged by satellites and screens
categories.description: Even at the end of the world, communication now leans on satellites, navigation systems, and networked devices that keep isolated people tied to wider systems. What you'll see: radio rooms, glowing monitors, tracked routes, weather updates, video calls, and digital maps guiding movement over empty white expanses.

categories.label: ice and wind setting every timetable
categories.description: In Antarctica, weather is not background but the chief condition of movement, safety, and work, with storms and cold able to halt plans at once. What you'll see: whiteouts, heavy parkas, creaking ice, grounded aircraft, delayed departures, and people waiting for a safe break in the wind.

categories.label: wildlife protected as part of shared stewardship
categories.description: Animals are treated less as local property than as species under international care, with strong expectations against harming breeding grounds and migration routes. What you'll see: penguin colonies, seals on the ice, marked-off areas, cautious human approach, and concern for returning animals to the right habitat.

categories.label: the south pole as remote world landmark
categories.description: The pole is both a real place and a symbolic destination, carrying the weight of exploration, endurance, and reaching the farthest edge of the map. What you'll see: ceremonial markers, endless white horizons, signs naming the pole, souvenir-style photo moments, and travel framed as arriving at Earth's extreme end.

categories.label: daily life built inside artificial shelter
categories.description: Because the open environment is hostile, ordinary living happens in heated interiors where food, sleep, work, and repair are carefully managed. What you'll see: bunk rooms, mess halls, humming generators, drying gear, thick doors, and people moving quickly between insulated buildings.

categories.label: air and sea routes replacing roads
categories.description: There are no ordinary continental roads linking settlements, so travel depends on aircraft, ships, and carefully plotted surface crossings over ice. What you'll see: ski-equipped planes, helicopters, icebreakers, snow vehicles, marked tracks, and journeys planned around fuel range and landing conditions.

categories.label: climate knowledge gathered from frozen landscapes
categories.description: Modern Antarctica is watched and studied as a key place for understanding weather, oceans, ice loss, and planetary change. What you'll see: measuring instruments, sample taking, observation towers, charts of temperature and wind, and a landscape treated as evidence as much as scenery.

categories.label: multinational visitors moving through a thin human footprint
categories.description: Human presence on the continent is sparse and temporary, with crews, researchers, and occasional visitors passing through small enclaves in a vast unpeopled setting. What you'll see: compact stations in huge emptiness, mixed accents and uniforms, shared equipment, and tiny clusters of human activity against ice and sea.
